questions.question: Why is dynamic programming called "RL with cheating"?
options: It peeks at the test set

You already KNOW the transition and reward functions — no sampling needed, just iterate the Bellman equation until V or π stops moving; model-free methods exist precisely for when you don't have that model
It uses more compute than allowed
It copies policies from a database
questions.explanation: Bellman designed these methods in 1957 and they still define correctness: "optimal policy for this MDP" means the policy DP would return.

questions.question: Why do you still need DP in 2026?
options: It's required for GPU drivers

Tabular research environments are solved with DP for gold-standard policies; exact values DEBUG sampling methods (if Q-learning disagrees with DP by 30%, Q-learning has a bug); and MCTS/AlphaZero/model-based RL all iterate Bellman backups over a model
Deep RL no longer works
Regulations mandate exact solutions
questions.explanation: DP is the reference implementation of the Bellman equation — the yardstick that makes every approximate method's error measurable.

questions.question: How do policy iteration and value iteration differ?
options: They are two names for one algorithm

Policy iteration alternates full evaluation (compute V^π to convergence) with greedy improvement, converging in ~5-20 outer loops; value iteration collapses both into one sweep of the Bellman OPTIMALITY backup (max over actions) — cheaper per iteration, more iterations
Value iteration requires no model
Policy iteration only works on grids
questions.explanation: Policy iteration's convergence is guaranteed because each improvement strictly increases V^π somewhere and the deterministic-policy space is finite.

questions.question: What is Generalized Policy Iteration (GPI)?
options: A specific 1957 algorithm
The unifying frame: value function and policy locked in a two-way improvement loop — async value iteration, Q-learning, actor-critic, and PPO are all instances driving both toward mutual consistency
Policy iteration with neural networks
A genetic programming variant
questions.explanation: GPI is why the rest of the phase rhymes: every algorithm is some schedule of "evaluate a bit, improve a bit" with different estimators plugged in.

questions.question: Why does γ < 1 guarantee convergence?
options: It makes rewards positive

The Bellman operator is a γ-contraction in sup-norm (||TV − TV'||∞ ≤ γ||V − V'||∞), which implies a unique fixed point and geometric convergence — drop it and you need a finite horizon or absorbing terminal state instead
Small numbers prevent overflow
It doesn't — convergence is empirical
questions.explanation: The contraction-mapping argument is the one theorem underwriting the whole phase: iterate any γ-contraction from any starting point and you land at the same unique answer.
